planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost for spring bloom; or sow in fall in mild climates (zones 7–9) for a winter-spring annual that blooms prolifically before summer heat arrives; a charming cottage garden annual that self-seeds reliably.

directions: Direct sow in early spring while nights are still cool, or in fall for zone 7–9 winter-spring bloom; barely cover seeds with soil and keep moist until germination. Sweet William catchfly (also called None-So-Pretty and Sweet William silene) is a delightful European annual producing slender, somewhat sticky stems with clusters of small, magenta-pink, five-petaled flowers with notched petals in tight, rounded clusters that bloom prolifically from spring through early summer; it strongly resembles sweet William but is a much more compact plant. The sticky glands on the stem trap small insects — hence 'catchfly' — but this causes no harm to the plant. An excellent self-seeding cottage garden annual that naturalizes nicely in gravel paths, rock gardens, and between pavers; butterflies and small native bees visit the flowers reliably. Direct sow in drifts for best effect.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in wet to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; a native wetland sedge ideal for rain gardens, pond edges, stream banks, and consistently moist shaded spots where most plants struggle.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in consistently wet to moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; awl-fruited sedge tolerates prolonged standing water and is an excellent choice for the lowest and wettest spots in rain gardens, bioswales, and naturalized wet areas. Carex stipata is a robust native sedge found throughout eastern North America in wet forests, swamps, and stream banks, producing coarse, upright, bright green, W-shaped stems (the distinctive spongy stem cross-section identifies many Carex species) 2–3 feet tall with prominent, straw-colored to brownish flower and fruit spikes in early summer; the awl-shaped perigynia (seed coverings) give it a spiky, somewhat bristly appearance at fruit set. Plant in masses for effective erosion control on wet slopes and stream banks; it provides important cover and seed for waterfowl and songbirds.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; never-never plant is an excellent tropical shade perennial for zone 9b–11 gardens or as a container plant brought indoors in winter in cooler zones — grown primarily for its stunning silver and dark green striped foliage.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; never-never plant is intolerant of drought, direct sun, and cold temperatures below 50°F. Ctenanthe setosa is a Brazilian rainforest native in the prayer plant family (Marantaceae) prized for its spectacular foliage: long, oval leaves with dramatic silver-grey stripes on a dark olive-green background and deep burgundy-purple undersides that flash when the leaves move in a breeze. Like other Marantaceae, the leaves fold upward at night in a sleep movement (nyctinasty) that gives the family its common name 'prayer plant.' Grow in deep, consistent shade with high humidity and regular moisture but excellent drainage; use as a bold tropical accent in shaded island beds in zone 10–11 or as a high-impact container plant for patios in cooler zones. Propagate by dividing clumps in spring.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost; a western North American native perennial with brilliant scarlet flowers that are outstanding for hummingbirds and are held on long sticky stems — the catchfly characteristics give it a distinctive tactile quality in the garden.

directions: Direct sow in spring, or start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost and transplant after all frost risk has passed; cardinal catchfly prefers dry to moderately moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade and is drought-tolerant once established. Silene laciniata is a native wildflower of the Pacific Coast ranges and Sierra Nevada foothills, producing long-stemmed, brilliant scarlet flowers with fringed (lacinate) petals on sticky, 12–36 inch stems from spring through fall; the vivid red flowers are a magnet for hummingbirds and are among the most spectacular of all native red wildflowers. It is more heat- and drought-tolerant than many Silene species and is suitable for dry woodland edges, rocky slopes, and naturalized gardens in the western US and in the drier parts of the Southeast. The sticky stems deter crawling insects from reaching the flowers.
sources: Las Pilitas Nursery Native Plant Guide

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; this Asian beautyberry is more compact than the native American beautyberry (C. americana) and produces incredibly vivid violet-purple berries that are among the most electric colors of any fall fruiting shrub.

directions: Plant in spring or early fall in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; purple beautyberry performs best in partial afternoon shade in zone 7–8 which prevents leaf scorch while maintaining prolific berry production. Callicarpa dichotoma is the Asian counterpart to the native American beautyberry, producing a more compact shrub (3–5 feet) with arching branches and, in fall, spectacular clusters of vivid violet-purple berries so intense in color they seem almost artificial — these berries are among the most electric and eye-catching of any fruiting shrub in the temperate garden and are invaluable for fall color. The small, lavender flowers in summer are attractive to pollinators. Plant in groups of 3 or more for best berry set; prune hard in late winter to encourage vigorous new growth and heavy fruiting. The berries are mildly toxic to humans.
